title: Informação Pessoal
subtitle: Personal Information
description: Learn to introduce yourself fully, use personal pronouns, and conjugate key verbs like ser (to be) and ter (to have).

vocabulary.example: Eu sou estudante. Tu és português?
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I am a student. Are you Portuguese?
vocabulary.tip: In Portuguese, subject pronouns are often omitted as the verb ending shows the person

vocabulary.example: Nós somos amigos. Eles são de Lisboa.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: We are friends. They are from Lisbon.
vocabulary.tip: In Portugal, 'vocês' is the standard plural 'you'. 'Vós' is archaic.

vocabulary.translation: To be (I am / you are / he is / we are / they are)
vocabulary.example: Eu sou portuguesa. Ela é espanhola.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I am Portuguese. She is Spanish.
vocabulary.tip: SER is for permanent characteristics: identity, nationality, profession, origin

vocabulary.example: Eu tenho 25 anos. Ela tem um cão.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I am 25 years old. She has a dog.
vocabulary.tip: Use TER + anos to express age in Portuguese

vocabulary.translation: To be called / To call oneself
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: What's your name? My name is João.
vocabulary.tip: This is a reflexive verb — the pronoun changes with the subject

vocabulary.example: — Qual é a tua nacionalidade? — Sou brasileiro.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: — What is your nationality? — I am Brazilian.
vocabulary.tip: Nationality adjectives agree in gender and number with the person described

vocabulary.example: — De onde és? — Sou do Brasil, de São Paulo.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: — Where are you from? — I'm from Brazil, from São Paulo.

vocabulary.example: — Quantos anos tens? — Tenho vinte e dois anos.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: — How old are you? — I am 22 years old.
vocabulary.tip: Literally: 'How many years do you have?' — TER is used for age

vocabulary.example: Eu sou portuguesa, sou de Braga.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: I am Portuguese, I'm from Braga.
vocabulary.tip: Nationality adjectives change for masculine/feminine and singular/plural

vocabulary.example: O Miguel é espanhol. A María é espanhola.
vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Miguel is Spanish. María is Spanish.
vocabulary.tip: Irregular nationality — the feminine adds -a, plural adds -is for m., -as for f.

grammar.title: Verb SER vs. TER & Personal Pronouns

sections.heading: Personal Pronouns in Portuguese
sections.body: Portuguese has a rich system of subject pronouns. Unlike English, the verb ending usually tells you who is performing the action, so pronouns are often dropped. The informal 'tu' is used with friends, family, and peers in Portugal. 'Vocês' is the standard plural 'you' (not 'vós', which is archaic). In formal situations, you may hear 'o senhor / a senhora'. Note that 'ele/ela' refer to people, while 'ele' can also refer to masculine objects in some contexts.

sections.heading: Subject Pronouns
rows: Used with friends, family, peers in Portugal
Also 'você' (formal you, but verb uses 3rd person)
Often replaced by 'a gente' in spoken Portuguese (uses 3rd person verb!)

sections.heading: The Verb SER — To Be (Permanent)
sections.body: SER is one of two Portuguese verbs meaning 'to be'. It is used for permanent or inherent characteristics: identity, nationality, profession, origin, physical descriptions, and time/date. SER is highly irregular — you must memorize its conjugation. Think of SER as describing what something IS at its core, fundamentally and permanently.

sections.heading: SER — Present Tense Conjugation
rows: Eu sou estudante. (I am a student.)
Ela é professora. (She is a teacher.)
Eles são de Lisboa. (They are from Lisbon.)

sections.heading: The Verb TER — To Have
sections.body: TER means 'to have' and is used for possession, age (in Portuguese you 'have' years, not 'are' years old), and many expressions. Unlike SER, TER is used for temporary states and concrete possessions. It is also irregular in the present tense. A very important use: TER + anos = to be X years old. You never use SER for age in Portuguese!

sections.heading: TER — Present Tense Conjugation
rows: Eu tenho 25 anos. (I am 25 years old.)
Tu tens um cão. (You have a dog.)
Ela tem uma casa. (She has a house.)
Eles têm dois filhos. (They have two children.)

items.portuguese: Eu sou brasileiro. Eu tenho trinta anos.
items.translation: I am Brazilian. I am thirty years old.
items.pronunciation: ay-oo soh brah-zee-LAY-roo. ay-oo TEN-yoo TREEN-tah ah-noosh.

items.portuguese: Ela é médica. Ela tem dois filhos.
items.translation: She is a doctor. She has two children.
items.pronunciation: EH-lah eh MEH-dee-kah. EH-lah tame doysh FEE-lyoosh.

items.portuguese: Nós somos de Portugal. Nós temos uma casa em Lisboa.
items.translation: We are from Portugal. We have a house in Lisbon.
items.pronunciation: nosh SOH-moosh deh poor-too-GAHL. nosh TAY-moosh OO-mah kah-zah eem LEESH-boh-ah.

sections.heading: The Reflexive Verb CHAMAR-SE
sections.body: CHAMAR-SE means 'to be called' or 'to call oneself'. It is a reflexive verb, meaning the action reflects back on the subject. The reflexive pronoun changes depending on the subject: 'chamo-me' (I call myself), 'chamas-te' (you call yourself), 'chama-se' (he/she calls himself/herself). This is the most natural way to say your name in Portuguese. Note: the verb part (cham-) also changes with the person, just like any regular -ar verb.

sections.heading: Nationalities & Gender Agreement
sections.body: In Portuguese, nationality adjectives must agree with the person they describe — both in gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural). Most nationalities follow predictable patterns: masculine ends in -o, -ês, -eu, or a consonant; feminine typically changes -o → -a, -ês → -esa, -eu → -eia. Plurals add -s. Some nationalities like 'português/portuguesa' are irregular — memorize them individually.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes & Tips
items: Never use SER for age! In Portuguese, you 'have' years: 'Tenho vinte anos' (I have 20 years), NOT 'Sou vinte anos'.
Don't confuse 'tu' and 'você'. In Portugal, 'tu' takes 2nd person verb forms (és, tens), while 'você' takes 3rd person forms (é, tem) even though it means 'you'.
After SER, you do NOT use an indefinite article before professions: 'Sou médico' (not 'Sou um médico'). But with adjectives, you do: 'Sou um bom médico'.
The verb TER has a special spelling in 'tenho' (I have) — the 'nh' makes a nasal 'ny' sound, like in 'canyon'.
'Nós' is increasingly replaced by 'a gente' in spoken Portuguese, but 'a gente' takes 3rd person verbs: 'A gente é de Lisboa' (not 'A gente somos').
When using CHAMAR-SE, the reflexive pronoun usually goes AFTER the verb connected by a hyphen: 'Chamo-me', not 'Me chamo'.

exercises.title: SER or TER?
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct verb to complete each sentence.

items.question: Eu ___ estudante de Economia.
items.explanation: Use SER for identity/profession. 'Eu sou estudante' = I am a student.

items.question: Ela ___ vinte e cinco anos.
items.explanation: Use TER for age. 'Ela tem vinte e cinco anos' = She is 25 years old.

items.question: Nós ___ de Lisboa.
items.explanation: Use SER for origin. 'Nós somos de Lisboa' = We are from Lisbon.

items.question: Eles ___ um cão e um gato.
items.explanation: Use TER for possession. 'Eles têm um cão e um gato' = They have a dog and a cat.

exercises.title: Conjugate SER or TER
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank with the correct form of SER or TER.

exercises.title: Build Sentences with CHAMAR-SE
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct reflexive form to complete each sentence.

items.hint: 2nd person — the pronoun goes before in questions
